---
title: "Merging Datasets with Predicted ML values for NA values"
author: "Rohan"
date: "2025-07-14"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r}
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(tibble)
library(dlookr)
library(dplyr)
library(sampling)
library(mice)
set.seed(123)
```


```{r}

# 1) Path to your CSV file:
dataset_path_1    <- "../Datasets/alzheimers_disease_data.csv"
dataset_path_2    <- "../Datasets/alzheimers_prediction_dataset.csv"


# 2) Name of the column you want to predict (the “target”):
target_variable_1 <- "Diagnosis"
target_variable_2 <- "Alzheimer.s.Diagnosis"

#this will be the global target variable name
target_variable <- "Alzheimer.s.Diagnosis"


# 3) Any columns to drop before analysis (e.g. IDs, metadata):
exclude_variable_1 <- c("DoctorInCharge", "PatientID")
exclude_variable_2 <- "Country"

# If you don't need to drop anything, set: exclude_variable <- character(0)

# 4) define your output folder relative to current wd
outdir <- "../All Reports/Merged Dataset Reports"
```

Creating user defined functions to Load and Inspect Data

```{r}

# Function to load a CSV file into a data.frame
load_data <- function(path) {
  read.csv(
    path,
    stringsAsFactors = TRUE,    # keep character columns as characters
    na.strings      = c("", "NA", " ")  # treat these as missing
  )
}

# Load and inspect using the configured path
df1 <- load_data(dataset_path_1)
df2 <- load_data(dataset_path_2)


```


The information below on what the values mean is taken from the Kaggle website where it is mentioned.
```{r}

# 1. Defining the Recoder
recode_df <- function(df, binary_vars, multi_specs) {
  # 1a) all 0/1 binaries → No/Yes
  df <- df %>%
    mutate(across(all_of(binary_vars),
                  ~ factor(.x, levels = c(0,1), labels = c("No","Yes"))))
  
  # 1b) each multi‑level var
  for (var in names(multi_specs)) {
    specs <- multi_specs[[var]]
    df[[var]] <- factor(df[[var]],
                        levels = specs$levels,
                        labels = specs$labels)
  }
  
  df
}

# 2. Specifying binary features for df1 
binary1 <- c(
  "Smoking", "FamilyHistoryAlzheimers", "CardiovascularDisease", "Diabetes",
  "Depression", "HeadInjury", "Hypertension", "MemoryComplaints",
  "BehavioralProblems", "Confusion", "Disorientation", "PersonalityChanges",
  "DifficultyCompletingTasks", "Forgetfulness", "Diagnosis"
)

# 3. Specifying multi‑level features for df1
multi1 <- list(
  Gender         = list(levels = c(0,1),     labels = c("Male","Female")),
  Ethnicity      = list(levels = c(0,1,2,3), labels = c("Caucasian","African American","Asian","Other")),
  EducationLevel = list(levels = c(0,1,2,3), labels = c("None","High School","Bachelor's","Higher"))
)

# 4. Applying it on df1
df1 <- recode_df(df1, binary1, multi1)

```

Dropping unwated columns/features
```{r}
# Drop the unwanted columns using the vectors defined above
# Drop function
drop_columns <- function(df, vars_to_drop = NULL) {
  if (!is.null(vars_to_drop) && length(vars_to_drop) > 0) {
    df <- df %>% select(-all_of(vars_to_drop))
  }
  df
}

df1 <- drop_columns(df1, exclude_variable_1)
df2 <- drop_columns(df2, exclude_variable_2)

```


Making the target variable the same name in both the dataframes
```{r}
# General function to rename one column
rename_variable <- function(df, old_name, new_name) {
  df %>% rename(!!new_name := !!sym(old_name))
}

# Usage for the target variable
df1 <- rename_variable(df1, target_variable_1, target_variable_2)
```

Diagnose Report for DF1 and DF2
```{r}
# 1. create it if it doesn't exist
if (!dir.exists(outdir)) {
  dir.create(outdir)
}

# 2. function to save a dlookr diagnose report with an “initial” prefix
save_diagnose_report <- function(df, name, prefix = "Initial") {
  report    <- df %>% diagnose()
  fname     <- paste0(prefix, "_", name, "_diagnose_report.csv")
  file_path <- file.path(outdir, fname)
  write.csv(report, file_path, row.names = FALSE)
  message("Saved report to ", file_path)
}

# 3. call it with meaningful names
save_diagnose_report(df1, "df1_alzheimers_disease_data")
save_diagnose_report(df2, "df2_alzheimers_prediction_dataset")

```

```{r}

save_summary_csv <- function(df, name, prefix = "Initial") {
  
  # 1) numeric summaries: min, Q1, median, mean, Q3, max
  num_vars  <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.numeric)]
  num_stats <- df %>%
    summarise(across(all_of(num_vars),
                     list(
                       min    = ~ min(.x,   na.rm = TRUE),
                       Q1     = ~ quantile(.x, .25, na.rm = TRUE),
                       median = ~ median(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
                       mean   = ~ mean(.x,   na.rm = TRUE),
                       Q3     = ~ quantile(.x, .75, na.rm = TRUE),
                       max    = ~ max(.x,   na.rm = TRUE)
                     ),
                     .names = "{col}_{fn}"
    )) %>%
    pivot_longer(
      cols      = everything(),
      names_to  = c("variable", "stat"),
      names_sep = "_"
    ) %>%
    pivot_wider(names_from = stat, values_from = value)
  
  # 2) factor summaries: number of levels + labels
  fac_vars  <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.factor)]
  fac_stats <- tibble(
    variable     = fac_vars,
    n_categories = map_int(fac_vars, ~ nlevels(df[[.x]])),
    categories   = map_chr(fac_vars, ~ paste(levels(df[[.x]]), collapse = ", "))
  )
  
  # 3) join into one table
  summary_tbl <- full_join(num_stats, fac_stats, by = "variable")
  
  # 4) write out
  fname <- paste0(prefix, "_", name, "_summary.csv")
  fpath <- file.path(outdir, fname)
  write.csv(summary_tbl, fpath, row.names = FALSE)
  message("Saved summary CSV to ", fpath)
}

# USAGE
save_summary_csv(df1, "df1_alzheimers_disease_data")
save_summary_csv(df2, "df2_alzheimers_prediction_dataset")
```


```{r}
# 1) A printer: just computes and prints to console  
print_column_comparison <- function(df1, df2) {
  common  <- intersect(names(df1), names(df2))
  unique1 <- setdiff(names(df1), names(df2))
  unique2 <- setdiff(names(df2), names(df1))
  
  cat("Common columns:\n");             print(common)
  cat("\nOnly in first dataframe:\n");  print(unique1)
  cat("\nOnly in second dataframe:\n"); print(unique2)
}

# 2) A saver: computes the same lists and writes them to CSV  
save_column_comparison <- function(df1, df2,
                                   prefix = "Initial") {
  
  common  <- intersect(names(df1), names(df2))
  unique1 <- setdiff(names(df1), names(df2))
  unique2 <- setdiff(names(df2), names(df1))
  
  write.csv(data.frame(column = common),
            file.path(outdir, paste0(prefix, "_common_features.csv")),
            row.names = FALSE)
  write.csv(data.frame(column = unique1),
            file.path(outdir, paste0(prefix, "_unique_to_df1.csv")),
            row.names = FALSE)
  write.csv(data.frame(column = unique2),
            file.path(outdir, paste0(prefix, "_unique_to_df2.csv")),
            row.names = FALSE)
  
  message("Saved column comparison CSVs to: ", outdir)
}

# Usage

# just print to console
print_column_comparison(df1, df2)

# then save to CSVs
save_column_comparison(df1, df2)

```


```{r}
# 1. User-Defined Define drop‐lists
drops_df1 <- c(
  # Drop df1’s less detailed Smoking variable since df2’s Smoking.Status is more informative
  "Smoking",
  # Drop df1’s binary Depression to keep only df2’s more informative Depression.Level
  "Depression"
)
drops_df2 <- c(
  # Drop df2’s Education.Level since df1’s EducationLevel is the preferred variable as df2’s Education.Level is vague
  "Education.Level"
)

# 2. User-Define rename‐maps
rename_map_df1 <- c(
  # Rename df1’s numeric alcohol variable for consistency
  "AlcoholConsumption" = "Alcohol.Consumption.NUM",
  # Rename df1’s numeric PhysicalActivity for consistency
  "PhysicalActivity"   = "Physical.Activity.NUM",
  # Rename diet variables: numeric in df1
  "DietQuality"        = "Diet.Quality.NUM",
  # Rename sleep variables: numeric in df1
  "SleepQuality"       = "Sleep.Quality.NUM"
)

rename_map_df2 <- c(
  # Rename df2’s categorical alcohol variable for consistency
  "Alcohol.Consumption"           = "Alcohol.Consumption.CAT",
  # Rename df2’s factor Physical.Activity.Level for consistency
  "Physical.Activity.Level"       = "Physical.Activity.CAT",
  # Rename diet variables: categorical in df2
  "Dietary.Habits"                = "Diet.Quality.CAT",
  # Rename sleep variables: categorical in df2
  "Sleep.Quality"                 = "Sleep.Quality.CAT",
  # Rename df2’s Family.History.of.Alzheimer.s to match df1’s FamilyHistoryAlzheimers
  "Family.History.of.Alzheimer.s" = "FamilyHistoryAlzheimers"
)

# 3. Apply drops and renames in loops which uses the existing drop_columns() and rename_variable() functions

# For df1
for (var in drops_df1) {
  df1 <- drop_columns(df1, var)
}
for (old in names(rename_map_df1)) {
  df1 <- rename_variable(df1, old, rename_map_df1[[old]])
}

# For df2
for (var in drops_df2) {
  df2 <- drop_columns(df2, var)
}
for (old in names(rename_map_df2)) {
  df2 <- rename_variable(df2, old, rename_map_df2[[old]])
}

# 4. using the function created earlier to check common and unique values for each dataframe
print_column_comparison(df1, df2)

# 6. then print with change in prefix name to distinguish initial and processed
save_column_comparison(df1, df2, 
                       prefix = "Processed")
```


```{r}
#Doing stratified sampling in a way that the df2 with 74283 rows are dialed down to 5000 rows with class balance
# 1. Generic stratified sampling function
stratified_sample <- function(df, strat_var, sizes, method = "srswor") {
  # df:            a data.frame
  # strat_var:     name of the factor column to stratify on (string)
  # sizes:         named numeric vector, e.g. c("Yes"=2500, "No"=2500)
  # method:        sampling method, default “srswor” (without replacement)
  
  # perform stratification
  strata_idx <- strata(
    df,
    stratanames = strat_var,
    size        = sizes,
    method      = method
  )
  
  # subset and return
  df[strata_idx$ID_unit, ]
}

# 2. Usage
# Defining how many per class (“Yes” vs “No”) to sample, keeping the same value for class balance
strata_sizes <- c("Yes" = 2500, "No" = 2500)

# Apply to df2
df2 <- stratified_sample(
  df  = df2,
  strat_var = target_variable,
  sizes = strata_sizes
)

# Check result
table(df2[[target_variable]])

```

```{r}

# Merge both dataframes, keeping all columns (unique and common)
df3 <- bind_rows(df1, df2)

#diagnostic report 
save_diagnose_report(df3, "Merged_Dataset")

# summary for the merged dataset saving in the folder as CSV
save_summary_csv(df3, "Merged_Dataset", prefix = "Initial")

```

```{r}

# MICE imputation function
impute_all <- function(df, m = 1, maxit = 5) {
  # 1) Identify numeric vs factor columns
  num_vars <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.numeric)]
  fac_vars <- names(df)[sapply(df, is.factor)]
  
  # 2) Build the 'methods' vector: "rf" for numerics, "polyreg" for factors
  methods <- c(
    setNames(rep("rf", length(num_vars)), num_vars),
    setNames(rep("polyreg", length(fac_vars)), fac_vars)
  )
  
  # 3) Run mice once on all columns that need imputing
  mids_obj <- mice(
    df[, names(methods)],  # only the columns we impute
    method    = methods,
    m         = m,
    maxit     = maxit,
    printFlag = FALSE
  )
  
  # 4) Extract and return the completed dataset
  complete(mids_obj, 1)
}

# Usage

# Impute your merged df3
df_final <- impute_all(df3, m = 1, maxit = 5)

```

```{r}
#Saving the newly created Merged Dataset as a CSV in our Datasets folder
write.csv(df_final, "../Datasets/Merged_Dataset.csv", row.names = FALSE)

#Diagnostics Report
save_diagnose_report(df_final, "Merged_Dataset", prefix = "Final")
#Summary Report
save_summary_csv(df_final, "Merged_Dataset", prefix = "Final")
```
